enrichment.short_description: LanguageTool is an open-source, AI-powered grammar, spelling and style checker supporting 30-plus languages across browsers, desktop apps and an API.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Premium from about $4.17/month billed every 2 years)
enrichment.seo_title: LanguageTool Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: LanguageTool review 2026: see how this open-source, multilingual grammar and style checker compares on features, pricing tiers and Grammarly alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: LanguageTool is an open-source, multilingual grammar, style and spell-checking platform that began in 2003 as a diploma-thesis project by German computational linguist Daniel Naber, who built the original rule-based checking engine and released it as free, open-source software; over the following two decades the project grew into a commercial company headquartered in Potsdam, Germany, employing roughly 29 people as a remote-first team, and in April 2023 LanguageTool was acquired by Learneo, an edtech holding company that also owns Course Hero and LitCharts, while the open-source core engine remains publicly available on GitHub. Architecturally, LanguageTool combines a large rule-based grammar engine (tens of thousands of hand-written and community-contributed rules) with statistical and neural machine-learning models for detecting style issues and word choice, and its most advanced paraphrasing and rewriting features are powered by generative AI; this hybrid approach differentiates it from purely neural competitors and lets the free, offline-capable core still catch a wide range of mistakes without a live AI backend. The product ships as a website checker, browser add-ons for Chrome, Firefox, Edge and Safari, desktop apps for Windows, macOS and Linux, mobile keyboards for Android and iOS, native integrations for Google Docs, Microsoft Word and Outlook, and OpenOffice/LibreOffice, alongside a developer-facing HTTP API and a self-hostable server package that lets businesses run LanguageTool on their own infrastructure for data-privacy reasons. LanguageTool checks grammar, spelling, punctuation and style across more than 30 languages and regional variants, each maintained largely by volunteer native-speaker linguists, and Premium subscribers unlock more than 20,000 additional checking rules, a 150,000-character-per-text limit (versus 2,000 characters free), unlimited AI-powered sentence paraphrasing (the free tier allows only a few paraphrases per day), a custom style guide for enforcing preferred terminology, and full access to every supported language. Pricing is structured around an Individual Premium plan billed in several cycles: monthly at roughly $24.90 (EUR19.90), quarterly at about $12/month, annually at about $7/month (EUR59.89/year), and a discounted two-year plan at around $4.17/month (EUR99.90 for two years); a Teams plan covers 2 to 20-plus members and layers on centralized billing, a shared Team Style Guide and a Team Dictionary for organization-specific terminology; and a Business tier serves larger organizations needing admin controls or on-premise deployment. The free Basic plan remains genuinely usable, covering core grammar and spelling checks with a 2,000-character limit and three free paraphrases per day, a more generous free offering than most rivals provide. LanguageTool's target audience spans individual writers, students and non-native English speakers who want multilingual support beyond English-only tools, plus businesses, publishers and software teams that need to embed grammar checking via API or want a self-hosted, privacy-compliant alternative to cloud-only competitors. Its most direct competitor is Grammarly, which has far greater brand recognition, a larger AI-writing feature set and deeper US-market penetration but is closed-source, English-centric, and has no true offline or self-hosted option; LanguageTool differentiates on open-source transparency, self-hosting for data-sensitive customers, and best-in-class support for European and non-English languages. Other competitors include ProWritingAid, which focuses on long-form style analytics popular with fiction authors; Ginger Software, which bundles translation and text-to-speech; Wordtune, which emphasizes AI rewriting over rule-based checking; and Microsoft Editor, bundled into Word and Outlook. LanguageTool's open-source roots continue to matter commercially: the community-maintained GitHub project feeds new rules and language support back into the commercial product, and organizations with strict data-residency requirements can deploy the engine on their own servers rather than sending text to a third-party cloud, a capability few closed-source competitors offer in the same way.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LanguageTool is a grammar, spelling and style checker that started as an open-source research project in 2003 and has since grown into a commercial product used by writers, students, businesses and developers who need multilingual proofreading beyond what English-only tools provide.

The platform blends thousands of hand-written grammar rules with machine-learning and generative-AI models, giving it both a reliable rule-based foundation and modern AI-powered rewriting and paraphrasing capabilities, all while keeping an open-source core engine publicly available.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: LanguageTool checks grammar, spelling, punctuation and style across more than 30 languages and regional variants, and ships as browser add-ons, desktop apps, mobile keyboards, document-editor integrations, and a developer API.

Premium unlocks over 20,000 additional style and grammar checks, a much higher character limit per document, unlimited AI paraphrasing, and a custom style guide for enforcing consistent terminology across a team or organization.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: LanguageTool offers a genuinely usable free Basic plan alongside an Individual Premium subscription billed monthly, quarterly, annually or every two years, with per-month cost dropping sharply the longer the commitment.

A Teams plan adds centralized billing, a shared style guide and dictionary for organizations with 2 to 20-plus members, while larger companies can opt for Business/Enterprise deployment including self-hosting options.

features.title: Multilingual grammar checking
features.description: Checks grammar, spelling and punctuation across 30-plus languages and regional variants, most maintained by volunteer native-speaker linguists.

features.title: AI-powered paraphrasing
features.description: Premium unlocks unlimited AI sentence rewriting to improve clarity, tone and style; the free plan allows a few paraphrases per day.

features.title: Browser and desktop integration
features.description: Available as add-ons for Chrome, Firefox, Edge and Safari, plus native desktop apps for Windows, macOS and Linux.

features.title: Custom style guide
features.description: Premium and Teams users can define preferred terminology and phrasing rules enforced consistently across documents.

features.title: Developer API and self-hosting
features.description: A REST API and self-hostable server package let businesses embed checking into their own products or run the engine on-premise for data privacy.

features.title: Team dictionary and management
features.description: Teams plans add a shared dictionary for technical terms, email-based member management, and centralized billing.

features.title: Document editor integrations
features.description: Works natively inside Google Docs, Microsoft Word, Outlook, and OpenOffice/LibreOffice.

features.title: Open-source core engine
features.description: The underlying rule-based engine is open source on GitHub, letting the community contribute rules and new language support.

pros: Genuinely usable free tier compared to most grammar-checker competitors
Strongest multilingual support in the category, covering 30-plus languages
Open-source core allows self-hosting for data-sensitive organizations
Two-year plan pricing is significantly cheaper than Grammarly's comparable tiers
Works across a wide range of browsers, desktop apps and document editors
cons: AI writing/rewriting features are less extensive than Grammarly's full suite
Interface and suggestions can feel less polished than newer AI-first competitors
Monthly billing is comparatively expensive versus the discounted annual/2-year plans
Some advanced style checks are limited to fewer languages than English
Team pricing is not transparently listed and may require inquiry for larger seats

faqs.question: Is LanguageTool free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, LanguageTool offers a free Basic plan with core grammar and spelling checks, a 2,000-character limit per text, and three free paraphrases per day.

faqs.question: How many languages does LanguageTool support?
faqs.answer: LanguageTool supports more than 30 languages and regional variants, including English, German, French, Spanish, Portuguese, Dutch, Italian, Polish, Russian, Ukrainian, Chinese and Japanese.

faqs.question: Is LanguageTool open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core rule-based checking engine is open source and available on GitHub, and it can be self-hosted by organizations that need on-premise deployment.

faqs.question: How does LanguageTool compare to Grammarly?
faqs.answer: LanguageTool offers broader multilingual support and a self-hosting option, while Grammarly has a larger AI-writing feature set and is more English-focused with wider brand recognition.

faqs.question: What is included in LanguageTool Premium?
faqs.answer: Premium adds over 20,000 additional grammar and style checks, a 150,000-character limit per text, unlimited AI paraphrasing, a custom style guide, and access to all supported languages.

faqs.question: Does LanguageTool offer a Teams plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Teams plans support 2 to 20-plus members with all Premium features plus a shared Team Style Guide, Team Dictionary, and centralized billing.

use_cases.title: Multilingual academic and professional writing
use_cases.description: Students and professionals writing in a non-native language use LanguageTool to catch grammar and style errors across dozens of languages that English-only checkers miss.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious enterprise proofreading
use_cases.description: Organizations with strict data-residency requirements self-host the open-source engine or use the API to embed checking into internal tools without sending text to third-party clouds.

use_cases.title: Team-wide style consistency
use_cases.description: Marketing and content teams use the Teams plan's shared style guide and dictionary to keep terminology and tone consistent across writers.

enrichment.short_description: LastPass is a cloud-based password manager that stores, generates and autofills passwords across devices, with tiers for individuals, families and businesses.
enrichment.seo_title: LastPass Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: LastPass review 2026: pricing tiers, security features, and how this cloud password manager compares to 1Password, Dashlane and Bitwarden.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: LastPass is a cloud-based password management and digital-vault platform founded in 2008 in Fairfax, Virginia by Joe Siegrist, Robert Billingslea and Sameer Kochar, who built the original browser-extension-driven password manager to solve the everyday problem of remembering dozens of unique account credentials; the company grew quickly on a freemium model and was acquired in 2015 by LogMeIn Inc. for approximately $110 million, after which LogMeIn itself rebranded as GoTo in 2022. LastPass operated as a GoTo-owned business unit for nearly a decade, during which it suffered a series of well-publicized security incidents, most notably a 2022 breach in which attackers stole encrypted customer vault backups from a cloud storage environment; in response the company overhauled its security architecture and executive leadership, and in 2024 GoTo formally spun LastPass off as an independent, standalone cybersecurity company headquartered on High Street in Boston, Massachusetts, with its own board and dedicated security-first leadership team. Architecturally, LastPass is built on a zero-knowledge encryption model: vault data is encrypted and decrypted locally on the user's device using a master password that LastPass itself never has access to, then the encrypted blob is synced across devices via LastPass's cloud infrastructure; the product ships as browser extensions for Chrome, Firefox, Edge and Safari, native apps for Windows and macOS, mobile apps for iOS and Android, and a web vault, with autofill, password generation, secure notes and encrypted file storage layered on top of the core vault. Beyond basic password storage, LastPass includes a security dashboard that scores password strength and reuse, dark web monitoring that alerts users if their credentials appear in known breach data, secure sharing of logins and notes, a built-in authenticator for generating time-based one-time passcodes, emergency access delegation, and for business tiers, a centralized admin console with dozens to 100-plus configurable security policies, SSO integration, directory sync, SCIM provisioning, and SaaS discovery/monitoring tools that flag unsanctioned application usage across an organization. Pricing spans six tiers: a Free plan limited to a single device type with unlimited password storage and basic sharing; Premium at around $3/month (billed annually) for full cross-device access and priority support; Families at around $4/month for up to six individual encrypted vaults plus a family manager dashboard and shared folders; and three business tiers billed per user per month - Teams at roughly $4/user/month for up to 50 seats with a basic admin console, Business at roughly $7/user/month for unlimited seats with 100-plus security policies and LastPass Families included as an employee perk, and Business Max at roughly $9/user/month adding SaaS Monitoring, SaaS Protect, unlimited SSO app integrations and advanced multi-factor authentication. Premium and Families plans include a 30-day free trial, while the business tiers offer a 14-day free trial. LastPass's target audience ranges from individual consumers and families who want a simple, cross-platform vault to mid-market and enterprise IT and security teams that need centralized credential governance, compliance reporting and SSO/MFA enforcement across an organization. Its principal competitors are 1Password, widely regarded as the more security-focused and polished alternative with strong Apple-ecosystem integration; Dashlane, which bundles a VPN and dark-web monitoring into higher tiers and markets heavily to SMBs; Bitwarden, an open-source password manager that undercuts LastPass on price, including a fully-featured free tier, and appeals to privacy-conscious and technical users who want to self-host; and Keeper Security, another enterprise-focused competitor emphasizing compliance certifications. LastPass differentiates itself primarily through brand longevity, breadth of platform support, and a deep business/IT-admin feature set, though its 2022 security incidents continue to be referenced by competitors and reviewers, making its post-spin-off emphasis on security-first leadership and infrastructure investment central to its current market positioning.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LastPass is one of the longest-running consumer and business password managers, founded in 2008 and now operating as an independent company after nearly a decade under GoTo/LogMeIn ownership and a 2024 spin-off.
The platform uses zero-knowledge encryption so vault data can only be decrypted locally with a user's master password, and it syncs encrypted vaults across browsers, desktop apps and mobile devices.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core features include password storage and generation, autofill, secure notes, dark web monitoring, and a security dashboard that flags weak or reused passwords.
Business tiers add a centralized admin console, configurable security policies, SSO and directory integration, SCIM provisioning, and SaaS application monitoring for IT and security teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: LastPass offers a free single-device-type plan alongside paid Premium and Families plans for individuals and households, all billed annually with free trial periods.
Three business tiers - Teams, Business and Business Max - scale per-user pricing with increasing security policy controls, SSO capabilities and SaaS monitoring features.

features.title: Zero-knowledge encrypted vault
features.description: Passwords are encrypted and decrypted locally with a master password LastPass never has access to.

features.title: Cross-device autofill
features.description: Browser extensions and mobile apps autofill saved logins across Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari, iOS and Android.

features.title: Dark web monitoring
features.description: Alerts users when their email or credentials appear in known data breaches.

features.title: Security dashboard
features.description: Scores password strength and flags reused or weak passwords across the vault.

features.title: Secure sharing
features.description: Share individual logins or shared folders with family members or teammates without exposing the plaintext password.

features.title: Admin console and policies
features.description: Business tiers include a centralized console with configurable security policies, SSO and directory sync.

features.title: Built-in authenticator
features.description: Generates time-based one-time passcodes (TOTP) for two-factor authentication without a separate app.

features.title: SaaS monitoring
features.description: Business Max detects unsanctioned or risky SaaS application usage across an organization.

pros: Free plan includes unlimited password storage
Long-established brand with broad platform and browser support
Deep business admin console and security policy controls
Family plan is competitively priced for up to 6 users
30-day free trial on Premium and Families plans
cons: Past security breaches (notably 2022) remain a lasting reputational concern
Free plan restricts users to a single device type
Business Max pricing is higher than some competitors' top tiers
Interface is viewed by some reviewers as less polished than 1Password
All paid plans require annual billing to get advertised per-month pricing

faqs.question: Is LastPass free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, LastPass offers a free plan with unlimited password storage, though it restricts access to a single device type.

faqs.question: How much does LastPass Premium cost?
faqs.answer: LastPass Premium costs around $3/month when billed annually, with a 30-day free trial available.

faqs.question: Is LastPass still owned by GoTo?
faqs.answer: No, LastPass was spun off from GoTo (formerly LogMeIn) into an independent, standalone company in 2024.

faqs.question: What happened in the LastPass security breaches?
faqs.answer: In 2022, attackers stole encrypted customer vault backups from a cloud storage environment; LastPass subsequently overhauled its security architecture and leadership.

faqs.question: What is the difference between LastPass Business and Business Max?
faqs.answer: Business Max adds SaaS Monitoring, SaaS Protect, unlimited SSO app integrations and advanced multi-factor authentication on top of the standard Business plan.

faqs.question: How many users can share a LastPass Families plan?
faqs.answer: LastPass Families supports up to six individual encrypted vaults under one family plan.

use_cases.title: Personal password management
use_cases.description: Individuals use LastPass to generate, store and autofill unique passwords across all their online accounts.

use_cases.title: Family credential sharing
use_cases.description: Households use the Families plan to give each member their own vault while sharing select logins like streaming or utility accounts.

use_cases.title: Enterprise credential governance
use_cases.description: IT and security teams use Business/Business Max to enforce password policies, SSO and SaaS usage monitoring across employees.

enrichment.short_description: Latenode is a no-code and low-code workflow automation platform combining a visual builder with full custom JavaScript, AI agents and 1,200+ integrations.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Pay-as-you-go usage-based pricing beyond free CPU-seconds)
enrichment.seo_title: Latenode Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Latenode review 2026: see how this code-friendly automation platform's CPU-second pricing, AI agents and 1,200+ integrations compare to Zapier and Make.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Latenode is a workflow automation platform founded in 2022 by a bootstrapped, remote-first team that says it draws on more than nine years of hands-on automation experience running its founders' own businesses; the company has not disclosed institutional venture funding and positions itself as an independently funded, product-led alternative built to address complaints long-time users have with Zapier's per-task pricing and n8n's self-hosting and maintenance burden. Architecturally, Latenode is a hybrid no-code/low-code automation engine: workflows are built visually as connected nodes, similar to Zapier, Make or n8n, but every node can be extended or replaced with full custom JavaScript, including the ability to import NPM packages directly inside a node, giving technical users the flexibility of a scripting environment without leaving the visual canvas; this lets a single platform serve non-technical operators who just want to connect apps as well as developers who need loops, conditional branching or bespoke API logic that off-the-shelf connectors do not support. The platform connects to more than 1,200 named apps and over 5,500 total integrations, and it embeds native access to large language models including GPT, Claude and Gemini directly inside workflow nodes, alongside a drag-and-drop AI-agent builder for constructing multi-step autonomous agents, a built-in headless browser for web scraping and browser automation, built-in NoSQL databases and global variables for storing workflow state, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) storage for grounding AI responses in a company's own documents. Enterprise-oriented capabilities include SOC 2 alignment, single sign-on, role-based access control and GDPR alignment. Latenode's pricing model is unusual in the automation category: rather than billing per 'operation' or 'task' the way Zapier and Make do, Latenode meters actual CPU execution seconds, so a workflow that finishes in five seconds costs the same regardless of how many nodes or steps it contains, which the company markets as making costs predictable and, at high volume, substantially cheaper than Zapier. The Free plan includes 10,000 CPU-seconds per month, up to five active workflows, one execution worker, a 3-minute execution time limit, 3-day execution history, and access to the full visual builder, all app integrations, an AI Code Copilot trial, the AI Agent node, one RAG storage instance, webhooks, browser automation and the built-in database - core platform capabilities are not locked behind higher tiers. The Pay-as-you-Go plan has no fixed monthly base fee and includes the same 10,000 free CPU-seconds, then charges on a declining tiered rate starting at $0.00012 per CPU-second for the next 90,000 seconds and dropping as low as $0.00005 per CPU-second beyond 5 million seconds per month, while also raising limits to unlimited workflows and executions, five execution workers, a 10-minute execution time limit and 30-day execution history; illustrative examples the company publishes include roughly $10.80/month for 100,000 short one-second workflow runs and roughly $83.80/month for 100,000 heavier ten-second runs. Optional add-ons let teams buy additional reserved execution workers, higher file-size limits, longer execution time windows, shorter polling intervals and extra RAG storage instances, while a separate 'Plug-n-Play' (PnP) token system, priced at $1 per token, pays for premium provider nodes such as certain AI models, file converters, enrichment tools, or persistent authenticated connections to platforms like Telegram, WhatsApp and LinkedIn. A custom Enterprise plan is available for organizations with larger compliance or volume needs. Latenode's target audience spans solo founders and growth/marketing teams automating repetitive tasks, technical operations and engineering teams that need custom logic beyond simple app-to-app triggers, and AI-forward teams building agentic workflows that combine LLM calls, data retrieval and multi-step orchestration. Its core competitors are Zapier, the market leader known for the broadest app catalog and simplicity but criticized for expensive per-task pricing at scale; Make (formerly Integromat), which offers a similarly visual, branching scenario builder popular with operations teams; and n8n, an open-source, self-hostable automation tool favored by developers who want full infrastructure control but must manage hosting and maintenance themselves. Latenode differentiates itself by combining n8n-style code flexibility with Zapier-style hosted simplicity and a transparent, execution-time-based pricing model designed to stay predictable as usage scales.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Latenode is a workflow automation platform, founded in 2022, that pairs a visual drag-and-drop builder with full custom JavaScript support inside any node, positioning itself between simple no-code tools and developer-only platforms like n8n.
The platform emphasizes predictable, execution-time-based pricing instead of per-task billing, along with native AI model access and agent-building tools built directly into the workflow canvas.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Latenode connects to 1,200+ apps and 5,500+ total integrations, and includes a headless browser for web scraping, built-in NoSQL databases, global variables, and RAG storage for grounding AI responses in company data.
An AI Agent node and drag-and-drop agent builder let teams construct multi-step autonomous workflows using GPT, Claude, Gemini and other models natively, without separate API setup.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Free plan includes 10,000 CPU-seconds per month with full access to the core platform, while the Pay-as-you-Go plan removes workflow limits and bills only for actual execution seconds on a declining tiered rate.

Optional add-ons cover extra execution workers, longer runtimes and larger file sizes, while a separate token system pays for premium provider nodes like certain AI models and messaging-platform connections.

features: Full visual builder and all app integrations

features.title: Visual builder with custom JavaScript
features.description: Every node can run full JavaScript with NPM packages, combining no-code simplicity with developer-grade flexibility.

features.title: 1,200+ app integrations
features.description: Connects to over 1,200 named apps and more than 5,500 total integrations.

features.title: Native AI model access
features.description: Built-in access to GPT, Claude, Gemini and 1,200+ AI models directly inside workflow nodes.

features.title: AI agent builder
features.description: Drag-and-drop builder for constructing multi-step autonomous AI agents and multi-agent orchestration.

features.title: Headless browser automation
features.description: Built-in headless browser enables web scraping and browser-based automation tasks.

features.title: Built-in database and RAG storage
features.description: NoSQL databases and retrieval-augmented generation storage let workflows store state and ground AI responses in company data.

features.title: CPU-second based pricing
features.description: Bills by actual execution time rather than per-operation or per-task, aiming for more predictable costs at scale.

features.title: Enterprise security
features.description: SOC 2 alignment, SSO, role-based access control and GDPR alignment for larger organizations.

pros: Execution-time pricing can be significantly cheaper than per-task competitors at scale
Full JavaScript and NPM support inside nodes for advanced logic
Core platform features are not restricted to higher-priced tiers
Built-in AI models, agent builder and RAG remove need for separate AI tooling
Generous free tier with 10,000 CPU-seconds per month
cons: Usage-based pricing can be harder to predict upfront than flat-rate plans
Newer platform (founded 2022) with a smaller community than Zapier or Make
Separate PnP token system adds a second cost dimension to track
No disclosed venture funding may raise questions about long-term scale for enterprise buyers
Company headquarters and size are not publicly disclosed

faqs.question: Is Latenode free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Latenode offers a free plan with 10,000 CPU-seconds per month, five active workflows, and access to the full visual builder and integrations.

faqs.question: How does Latenode's pricing differ from Zapier or Make?
faqs.answer: Latenode bills by actual CPU execution seconds rather than per operation or task, so a workflow's cost depends on how long it runs rather than how many steps it contains.

faqs.question: Can I write custom code in Latenode?
faqs.answer: Yes, every node supports full JavaScript with NPM package imports, allowing custom logic, loops and conditional branching beyond standard no-code connectors.

faqs.question: Does Latenode support AI agents?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes a drag-and-drop AI agent builder plus native access to GPT, Claude, Gemini and other models directly inside workflow nodes.

faqs.question: What are Latenode's PnP tokens?
faqs.answer: Plug-n-Play tokens, priced at $1 each, pay for premium provider nodes such as certain AI models, file converters, and authenticated connections to platforms like Telegram or WhatsApp.

faqs.question: Who are Latenode's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Latenode competes primarily with Zapier, Make and n8n in the workflow automation space.

use_cases.title: Cost-sensitive high-volume automation
use_cases.description: Teams running large volumes of automated workflows use Latenode's execution-time pricing to avoid the per-task costs that make Zapier expensive at scale.

use_cases.title: Custom logic and API integration
use_cases.description: Developers use built-in JavaScript support to add loops, conditionals and bespoke API calls that standard no-code connectors cannot handle.

use_cases.title: AI agent and RAG workflows
use_cases.description: AI-forward teams build multi-step agents that call LLMs, retrieve data via RAG, and take automated actions across connected apps.

enrichment.short_description: Later is a social media scheduling and marketing platform that helps brands plan, auto-publish and analyze content across Instagram, TikTok, Facebook and more.
enrichment.seo_title: Later Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Later review 2026: pricing plans, scheduling features and Linkin.bio tools compared against Hootsuite, Buffer and Sprout Social alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Later is a social media marketing and scheduling platform that traces its roots to Latergramme, an Instagram-only scheduling tool co-founded in 2013 in Vancouver, Canada by Cindy Chen, Ian MacKinnon, Roger Patterson and Matt Smith to solve the lack of an official Instagram scheduling API at the time; the company rebranded to Later as it expanded beyond Instagram to Facebook, Pinterest, X, TikTok, LinkedIn, YouTube and Snapchat, and it has since raised a reported $270 million across nine funding rounds, including a $135 million equity round in April 2022, growing into a company headquartered in Vancouver with additional offices in London and New York. In 2024, Later merged with Mavrck, an influencer-marketing platform, to form a single unified business spanning both organic social scheduling and influencer/creator campaign management, positioning Later as a broader social marketing suite rather than a pure scheduling tool. Architecturally, Later is a cloud-based, multi-platform publishing and analytics engine organized around the concept of a 'social set' - a bundle of up to eight connected social profiles - with a visual drag-and-drop content calendar, a Linkin.bio landing-page tool that turns an Instagram grid into a shoppable, clickable microsite, bulk and auto-scheduling, a media library for organizing assets, and built-in analytics that report engagement, best-time-to-post recommendations, and, on higher tiers, competitive benchmarking against other brands. Later has increasingly layered generative-AI functionality into the product via 'AI credits,' letting users generate captions, hashtag suggestions and content ideas from within the calendar, with each plan tier allotting a different monthly AI-credit balance. The product ships as a web application and native mobile apps for iOS and Android, and includes two-factor authentication and role-based collaboration tools for teams working across multiple brands or clients. Pricing is structured in three core tiers, billed either monthly or at a discounted annual rate advertised as roughly three months free: Starter, at approximately $18.75/month annually, includes one social set (eight profiles), a single user, 30 scheduled posts per profile per month, five AI credits and up to three months of historical analytics; Growth, Later's most popular plan at roughly $37.50/month annually, doubles capacity to two social sets (16 profiles) and two users, raises the posting limit to 180 posts per profile per month, includes 50 AI credits, adds multi-profile scheduling and team collaboration tools, and extends analytics history to a full year; and Scale, at roughly $82.50/month annually, provides six social sets (48 profiles), four users, unlimited scheduled posts, 100 AI credits, custom analytics and competitive benchmarking, and up to two years of historical data. All tiers allow add-on purchases of extra social sets, extra users and additional AI credits. Later has historically also offered a limited free plan aimed at creators posting a small number of times per month, though it is no longer prominently marketed on the main pricing page, with the company instead emphasizing a trial period on paid plans. Later's target audience spans solo creators and small businesses that need affordable, visual-first Instagram and TikTok scheduling, up through marketing teams and agencies managing multiple brands or client accounts that need collaboration tools, approval workflows and deeper analytics. Its main competitors are Hootsuite, the long-established enterprise social media management platform with the broadest feature set and highest price point; Buffer, known for a simpler interface and lower starting price aimed at solo creators and small teams; Sprout Social, which targets larger, analytics-heavy organizations with comprehensive reporting and social listening capabilities; and Agorapulse and Sendible, which compete on agency-oriented multi-client workflows. Later differentiates itself primarily through its visual-first, Instagram/TikTok-native design heritage, the bundled Linkin.bio tool, and, post-Mavrck-merger, native influencer and creator campaign management that pure scheduling tools like Buffer do not offer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Later began in 2013 as Latergramme, one of the earliest Instagram scheduling tools, and has since grown into a full social media marketing platform covering Instagram, TikTok, Facebook, Pinterest, LinkedIn, YouTube and Snapchat.
Following its 2024 merger with influencer-marketing platform Mavrck, Later now spans both organic content scheduling and influencer/creator campaign management under one unified platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Later's visual content calendar, bulk scheduling and Linkin.bio shoppable-link tool remain central to its Instagram-first heritage, alongside analytics, best-time-to-post recommendations and competitive benchmarking on higher tiers.
AI credits built into every plan let users generate captions and content ideas directly inside the calendar, while collaboration tools support teams managing multiple brands or client accounts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Later's three main tiers - Starter, Growth and Scale - scale by number of connected social profiles ('social sets'), team seats, monthly post volume, AI credits and analytics history, billed monthly or at a discounted annual rate.
Add-ons let teams purchase extra social sets, users and AI credits beyond a plan's included allotment as their social presence grows.

features: Competitive benchmarking, up to 2 years of analytics

features.title: Visual content calendar
features.description: Drag-and-drop scheduling calendar for planning and auto-publishing posts across connected social profiles.

features.title: Linkin.bio
features.description: Turns an Instagram grid into a clickable, shoppable landing page linking each post to a destination URL.

features.title: Multi-platform publishing
features.description: Schedules content to Instagram, Facebook, TikTok, Threads, YouTube, Pinterest, LinkedIn and Snapchat.

features.title: AI content generation
features.description: AI credits let users generate captions, hashtags and content ideas directly inside the scheduling calendar.

features.title: Analytics and benchmarking
features.description: Reports engagement and best-time-to-post recommendations, with competitive benchmarking on the Scale plan.

features.title: Team collaboration
features.description: Multi-user access and collaboration tools for agencies and teams managing multiple brand accounts.

features.title: Influencer and creator campaigns
features.description: Following the Mavrck merger, Later supports influencer discovery and creator campaign management alongside organic scheduling.

features.title: Mobile apps
features.description: Native iOS and Android apps let users manage scheduling and review content on the go.

pros: Strong visual-first design well suited to Instagram and TikTok content
Linkin.bio adds shoppable-link functionality not bundled by all competitors
Post-Mavrck merger adds native influencer marketing capabilities
AI credits included on every paid plan for content generation
Tiered social-set structure scales cleanly for agencies managing multiple clients
cons: No prominently advertised free plan on the current pricing page
Analytics depth is generally viewed as less comprehensive than Sprout Social
Add-on costs for extra social sets and users can add up for larger teams
Starter plan's single social set may be limiting for multi-platform brands
Pricing has shifted over time, creating some confusion around current free-tier availability

faqs.question: Does Later have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Later has historically offered a limited free plan for creators posting infrequently, though it is not prominently featured on the current pricing page, which instead emphasizes paid plans with a trial period.

faqs.question: What is a 'social set' in Later's pricing?
faqs.answer: A social set is a bundle of up to eight connected social media profiles; plans scale by how many social sets, users, and posts per profile are included.

faqs.question: What social platforms does Later support?
faqs.answer: Later supports Instagram, Facebook, TikTok, Threads, YouTube, Pinterest, LinkedIn and Snapchat.

faqs.question: What is Linkin.bio?
faqs.answer: Linkin.bio is Later's tool that turns an Instagram profile's single bio link into a clickable, shoppable landing page tied to individual posts.

faqs.question: Is Later good for agencies?
faqs.answer: Yes, higher tiers like Growth and Scale include multiple users, multiple social sets, and collaboration tools suited to agencies managing several client accounts.

faqs.question: How does Later differ from Buffer or Hootsuite?
faqs.answer: Later has a more visual, Instagram/TikTok-first design and includes Linkin.bio and influencer marketing tools, while Hootsuite and Sprout Social generally offer broader analytics and enterprise features.

use_cases.title: Visual-first Instagram and TikTok scheduling
use_cases.description: Creators and brands use Later's visual calendar and Linkin.bio to plan grid layouts and drive traffic from Instagram bios.

use_cases.title: Multi-client agency management
use_cases.description: Social media agencies use multiple social sets and collaboration tools to manage scheduling across several client brands from one account.

use_cases.title: Influencer and creator campaigns
use_cases.description: Brands use Later's Mavrck-derived tools to discover creators, run influencer campaigns, and manage content alongside organic scheduling.

enrichment.short_description: Lattice is a people management platform for performance reviews, goal-setting and OKRs, engagement surveys and career growth used by HR and people teams.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing (modules from about $4/seat/month; $4,000 minimum annual spend)
enrichment.seo_title: Lattice Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lattice review 2026: pricing modules, performance and OKR features, and how this people-management platform compares to Culture Amp and 15Five.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Lattice is a people-management and performance platform founded in 2015 in San Francisco by Jack Altman and Eric Koslow, built originally around continuous performance reviews and goal-setting as an alternative to once-a-year, spreadsheet-driven HR review cycles; the company went through Y Combinator and grew into one of the best-known 'people ops' companies, raising a total of roughly $329.3 million in venture funding, including a $175 million Series F in January 2022 led by Dragoneer Investment Group with participation from Tiger Global, Founders Fund, Thrive Capital, Shasta Ventures and Khosla Ventures, as well as individual investors such as Salesforce CEO Marc Benioff and Reddit co-founder Alexis Ohanian, which tripled the company's valuation to about $3 billion. Jack Altman (brother of OpenAI CEO Sam Altman) stepped down as Lattice's CEO in 2024 while remaining chairman of the board, and in 2025 Lattice announced it would sunset the HRIS and payroll products it had expanded into, choosing instead to refocus on its original core strength of performance management, goals and engagement rather than compete as a full human-resources-information-system provider. Architecturally, Lattice is a cloud-based SaaS platform organized around modular products that share a common data layer: 1:1 meeting notes, weekly check-ins, peer feedback and Q&A boards run underneath all products as connective tissue, while an AI Agent - rolled out to all customers at no additional cost - sits across the platform to auto-generate meeting notes, coaching suggestions, review drafts, and plain-language analytics queries that return charts pulled from Lattice's Analytics engine. The core purchasable modules are Performance (structured performance reviews, succession planning, promotion and PIP workflows, calibration and talent review tools), Goals and OKRs (cascading company-to-individual goal trees, progress tracking, and integrations with tools like Salesforce and Jira), and Engagement (pulse surveys, onboarding/exit surveys, eNPS tracking, and AI-generated trend analysis), each of which can be purchased individually or combined, plus two add-on modules: Compensation (comp bands, benchmarking, and multi-step approval workflows for pay cycles) and Grow (competency frameworks, individual development plans, and career-track mapping). Pricing is per-seat, billed annually in USD only, with a $4,000 minimum annual contract value and dedicated human support included on every contract; list prices are approximately $10/seat/month for Performance, $8/seat/month for Goals and OKRs, $4/seat/month for Engagement, plus $6/seat/month for the Compensation add-on and $4/seat/month for the Grow add-on, while the most popular bundled option, Foundations (Performance plus Goals and OKRs together), lists at roughly $13/seat/month. There is no self-serve free trial; prospective customers must go through a sales-assisted demo process to see pricing tailored to their headcount and module mix. Lattice's target customers are mid-market to enterprise HR and people-operations teams, typically at companies from roughly 50 to several thousand employees, that want a unified system for performance reviews, OKRs and engagement surveys rather than stitching together point solutions, with particular strength among fast-growing technology companies that adopted Lattice during its early YC-network growth phase. Its principal competitors include Culture Amp, generally viewed as stronger on engagement analytics and people-science research; 15Five, which competes more directly on lightweight, manager-friendly continuous performance check-ins at a lower price point; Leapsome, which emphasizes tightly integrated OKRs and structured 1:1s; and Betterworks, which focuses specifically on enterprise goal-management and OKR software. Lattice differentiates itself through its combined performance-plus-engagement-plus-goals suite under one platform, its AI Agent embedded directly into manager workflows, and its pedigree as one of the original companies that popularized continuous performance management as a category, even as it has narrowed its scope away from full HRIS/payroll competition with providers like Rippling, Gusto and Workday.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lattice is a modular people-management platform, founded in 2015, built around continuous performance reviews, goal-setting and employee engagement rather than a once-a-year review cycle.

After expanding into full HR-information-system and payroll products, Lattice announced in 2025 it would sunset those offerings to refocus specifically on its original performance management strengths.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core modules cover Performance reviews and calibration, Goals and OKRs, and Engagement surveys, all connected through shared 1:1 notes, weekly check-ins and feedback tools.
An AI Agent, included free for all customers, auto-generates meeting notes, coaching suggestions, review drafts and plain-language analytics charts across the platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Lattice prices each module separately on a per-seat, annually billed basis, with a $4,000 minimum annual contract, and modules can be combined or purchased individually.
The Foundations bundle, combining Performance and Goals and OKRs, is the most popular pre-configured package, with Compensation and Grow available as additional add-on modules.

features.title: Performance reviews and calibration
features.description: Structured review cycles, succession planning, promotion and PIP workflows, and calibration sessions for managers.

features.title: Goals and OKRs
features.description: Cascading company-to-individual goal trees with progress tracking and integrations like Salesforce and Jira.

features.title: Engagement surveys
features.description: Pulse, onboarding and exit surveys with eNPS tracking and AI-generated trend analysis.

features.title: Lattice AI Agent
features.description: Built into 1:1s and available at no extra cost, generating meeting notes, coaching insights, review drafts and analytics charts from plain-language queries.

features.title: 1:1s and feedback tools
features.description: Shared meeting notes, weekly updates, peer feedback and Q&A boards underpin every module.

features.title: Compensation management (add-on)
features.description: Comp bands, benchmarking data, cycle management and multi-step approval workflows for pay decisions.

features.title: Career growth tools (add-on)
features.description: Competency frameworks, individual development plans and career-track mapping.

features.title: Analytics and integrations
features.description: Cross-module analytics engine plus integrations with common HR, calendar and productivity tools.

pros: Modular pricing lets companies buy only the products they need
AI Agent is included free across the platform rather than gated to higher tiers
Strong brand recognition and long track record in continuous performance management
Foundations bundle offers a cost-efficient combination of the two most-used modules
Dedicated human support included on every contract
cons: No self-serve free trial; requires a sales demo to see pricing
$4,000 minimum annual spend may be high for very small teams
USD-only billing can complicate international purchasing
Sunsetting HRIS and payroll narrows Lattice's role to performance-adjacent HR needs only
Per-seat, per-module pricing can become expensive as companies add multiple modules

faqs.question: Does Lattice offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: No, Lattice does not offer a self-serve free trial; prospective customers go through a sales-assisted demo to get pricing tailored to their team.

faqs.question: What is Lattice's Foundations plan?
faqs.answer: Foundations bundles the Performance and Goals and OKRs modules together at a discounted combined price and is Lattice's most popular pre-configured package.

faqs.question: Is Lattice still offering HRIS and payroll?
faqs.answer: No, Lattice announced in 2025 that it would sunset its HRIS and payroll products by mid-2026 to refocus on performance management, goals and engagement.

faqs.question: How is Lattice priced?
faqs.answer: Lattice is priced per seat per month, billed annually, with each module (Performance, Goals and OKRs, Engagement) purchasable individually or combined, plus optional Compensation and Grow add-ons.

faqs.question: What is the Lattice AI Agent?
faqs.answer: The Lattice AI Agent is built into 1:1 meetings and other workflows, generating notes, coaching suggestions, review drafts and plain-language analytics charts, included free for all customers.

faqs.question: Who are Lattice's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Lattice's main competitors include Culture Amp, 15Five, Leapsome and Betterworks.

use_cases.title: Company-wide OKR tracking
use_cases.description: Leadership and managers cascade company goals down to team and individual OKRs with progress tracking integrated into existing tools.

enrichment.short_description: LaunchDarkly is a feature management and experimentation platform that lets engineering teams control feature flags, rollouts and AI agent behavior at runtime.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Developer plan); Foundation from $10/month per service connection
enrichment.seo_title: LaunchDarkly Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: LaunchDarkly review 2026: feature flag and AI agent control pricing, key features, and how it compares to Split, Optimizely and open-source alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: LaunchDarkly is a feature-management and runtime-control platform founded in 2014 in Oakland, California by Edith Harbaugh and John Kodumal, two Harvey Mudd College classmates who identified the same problem from opposite vantage points - Harbaugh as a product manager at TripIt, Kodumal as an engineer at Atlassian who had built an internal feature-flagging system - and concluded that small and mid-sized engineering teams needed an externally hosted alternative to the custom feature-flag infrastructure that only companies like Amazon, Facebook and Netflix could afford to build in-house; the company was reportedly rejected by Y Combinator and by roughly 30 venture capital firms before finding early traction with developers outside Silicon Valley. LaunchDarkly went on to raise approximately $330 million in total funding, including a $200 million Series D in August 2021 led in part by Bessemer Venture Partners, Redpoint Ventures and 500 Global that tripled its valuation to roughly $3 billion, making it a feature-management unicorn roughly seven years after founding; the company now employs around 640 people and remains headquartered in Oakland. Architecturally, LaunchDarkly's core product, CodeControl, works by wrapping conditional logic in a customer's application code inside feature flags evaluated through LaunchDarkly's SDKs, available for roughly 30 languages and frameworks; flag state and targeting rules are distributed to a global streaming infrastructure so that flags can be toggled, rolled out to specific user segments, or instantly rolled back without a code redeploy, and the platform layers in A/B testing and experimentation, session replay, and audit-logged approval workflows for regulated environments. In response to the rise of AI-native software, LaunchDarkly has extended the same runtime-control model into a companion product called AgentControl, designed to govern the behavior of AI agents and LLM-powered features in production - tracking 'AI runs' (each LLM request or automated online evaluation counts as one run), enabling kill-switches, guardrails and staged rollouts for AI features the same way CodeControl does for traditional code. LaunchDarkly's pricing spans four tiers under a combined 'Full Platform' (CodeControl plus AgentControl) structure: a free Developer plan offers unlimited seats, 10 million logs and traces, unlimited feature flags, A/B tests and experiments, 5,000 session replays/error events, unlimited playgrounds and offline evaluations, 5,000 AI runs per month, and 14-day data retention, with no credit card required; the Foundation plan, billed yearly, adds user/account/device targeting, unlimited projects, single sign-on and 30-day retention, priced around $10/month per service connection plus roughly $8.33/month per 1,000 client-side monthly active users for CodeControl, with AgentControl overage priced at $5 per 1,000 AI runs beyond the free 5,000/month allotment; Enterprise is custom-priced and adds advanced targeting, release automation, scheduling and approval workflows, SAML/SCIM, custom roles, and 100-plus day retention; and the top-tier Guardian plan is also custom-priced and adds automated release monitoring, guardrail metrics, proactive failure alerts, and automatic pause-or-rollback of risky releases, aimed at the most safety-critical deployment environments. LaunchDarkly's target audience is primarily software engineering and platform teams at technology companies of all sizes, from startups on the free Developer tier to large enterprises running thousands of flags across regulated industries such as finance and healthcare that need audit trails and staged, reversible releases; more recently the AgentControl product also targets AI/ML platform teams that need production guardrails around autonomous or LLM-driven features. Its main competitors include Split (a feature-flagging and experimentation platform acquired by Harness), Optimizely (which combines feature flagging with broader digital experience experimentation), open-source alternatives Flagsmith and Unleash that appeal to teams wanting self-hosted control, and lighter-weight tools like ConfigCat; LaunchDarkly differentiates itself through the maturity and scale of its streaming flag-delivery infrastructure, its enterprise compliance certifications, and its early, dedicated move into governing AI-agent behavior as a distinct product line rather than an add-on feature.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LaunchDarkly, founded in 2014 by Edith Harbaugh and John Kodumal, pioneered feature flags as a hosted service, letting engineering teams toggle features, roll out to segments, and roll back instantly without redeploying code.
The platform has since expanded from CodeControl (traditional feature flag management) into AgentControl, a companion product for governing AI agent and LLM behavior in production.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include unlimited feature flags, user/account/device targeting, A/B testing and experimentation, session replay, and SDKs for roughly 30 languages and frameworks.
AgentControl extends the same runtime-control model to AI features, tracking 'AI runs' and enabling kill-switches, guardrails and staged rollouts for LLM-powered functionality.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: A free Developer plan offers unlimited seats and generous flag, log and AI-run limits with no credit card required, making LaunchDarkly accessible to small teams and individual developers.
Paid tiers - Foundation, Enterprise and Guardian - add SSO, release automation, advanced targeting, compliance certifications and automated release monitoring as organizations scale.

pricing_plans.price: From $10/month per service connection + $8.33/month per 1K client-side MAU

features.title: Feature flag management
features.description: Toggle features, roll out to targeted user segments, and instantly roll back without a code redeploy.

features.title: A/B testing and experimentation
features.description: Run controlled experiments tied directly to feature flags to measure impact before full rollout.

features.title: AgentControl for AI features
features.description: Governs AI agent and LLM-powered feature behavior in production, tracking AI runs and enabling kill-switches and guardrails.

features.title: Broad SDK support
features.description: SDKs available for roughly 30 languages and frameworks for both server-side and client-side flag evaluation.

features.title: Session replay and observability
features.description: Session replay and error tracking help teams understand user impact of flagged releases.

features.title: Release automation and approvals
features.description: Enterprise tiers add scheduling, approval workflows and audit logging for regulated release processes.

features.title: Automatic rollback (Guardian)
features.description: Guardian tier monitors guardrail metrics and can automatically pause or roll back risky releases.

features.title: Single sign-on and SCIM
features.description: Foundation and above add SSO, with Enterprise/Guardian adding SAML/SCIM for identity governance.

pros: Generous free Developer plan with unlimited seats and flags
Mature, high-scale streaming infrastructure for flag delivery
Dedicated AgentControl product for governing AI/LLM features in production
Strong enterprise compliance and audit capabilities
Broad SDK language and framework coverage
cons: Foundation and above pricing can become complex with multiple usage dimensions (service connections, MAUs, AI runs)
Enterprise and Guardian tiers require custom quotes rather than transparent pricing
Advanced release automation and guardrails are reserved for the highest-priced tiers
Learning curve for teams new to feature-flag-driven development practices
AgentControl is a newer product line with a shorter track record than CodeControl

faqs.question: Is LaunchDarkly free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Developer plan is free with unlimited seats, unlimited feature flags, 10 million logs and traces, and 5,000 AI runs per month, with no credit card required.

faqs.question: What is AgentControl?
faqs.answer: AgentControl is LaunchDarkly's product for governing AI agent and LLM-powered feature behavior in production, tracking AI runs and enabling guardrails, kill-switches and staged rollouts.

faqs.question: How is LaunchDarkly's Foundation plan priced?
faqs.answer: Foundation is billed yearly at around $10/month per service connection plus roughly $8.33/month per 1,000 client-side monthly active users, with AI run overages billed separately.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Enterprise and Guardian plans?
faqs.answer: Guardian includes everything in Enterprise plus automated release monitoring, guardrail metrics, proactive failure notifications and automatic pause-or-rollback of risky releases.

faqs.question: Who founded LaunchDarkly?
faqs.answer: LaunchDarkly was founded in 2014 by Edith Harbaugh and John Kodumal in Oakland, California.

faqs.question: Who are LaunchDarkly's main competitors?
faqs.answer: LaunchDarkly's main competitors include Split, Optimizely, and open-source alternatives like Flagsmith and Unleash.

use_cases.title: Safe, incremental feature rollouts
use_cases.description: Engineering teams use feature flags to release code to production and gradually roll out to user segments without redeploying.

use_cases.title: A/B testing and experimentation
use_cases.description: Product teams run controlled experiments tied to feature flags to measure the impact of new features before full release.

use_cases.title: Governing AI agent behavior in production
use_cases.description: AI/ML platform teams use AgentControl to add guardrails, kill-switches and staged rollouts for LLM-powered features.

enrichment.short_description: Lavender is an AI email coaching assistant that scores, rewrites and optimizes sales emails in real time inside Gmail, Outlook and CRMs.
enrichment.seo_title: Lavender Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lavender review 2026: AI email coaching features, pricing plans, and how this sales email assistant compares to Yesware, Outreach and Salesloft.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Lavender is an AI-powered email coaching platform for sales teams, founded in 2020 and headquartered in New York, built to give sellers real-time, data-driven feedback on cold emails and outreach messages the way a sales coach would review a call. Lavender created what its founders describe as the 'email intelligence' category and raised a total of about $13.2 million in venture funding, starting with a $2.2 million seed round led by Signia Venture Partners with participation from CapitalX and Position Ventures, followed roughly a year later by an $11 million Series A led by Norwest Venture Partners with continued participation from Signia; the company has grown to roughly 77 employees and counts customers including Clari, Lucidworks, Segment, Sendoso, Sharebite and Twilio. Architecturally, Lavender is delivered primarily as a browser extension and email-client plugin that overlays directly on Gmail, Outlook, and major sales-engagement and CRM platforms such as Salesloft, Outreach, HubSpot and Salesforce; as a rep drafts an email, Lavender's AI analyzes the copy in real time and returns an overall email score along with specific, line-by-line suggestions covering readability, tone, length, personalization, spelling and grammar, and spam-trigger phrases that could hurt deliverability, functioning less like a generic grammar checker and more like a sales-specific writing coach trained on patterns from millions of analyzed emails. Beyond live scoring, Lavender includes AI-generated email and subject-line rewrites, automated prospect and company research pulled into the compose window so reps do not have to tab out to LinkedIn or a company website, team and manager-facing analytics dashboards that show coaching trends and email-quality benchmarks across a sales org, and integrations that let managers identify which reps and message patterns are driving replies versus getting ignored. Pricing follows a freemium structure: the free plan is capped at a small number of monthly email analyses and personalizations (around five of each) alongside basic scoring features, intended as a trial-like entry point rather than a fully usable free tier for active sellers; the Starter individual plan runs roughly $27 to $29 per month and unlocks full, unlimited access to the core AI writer, real-time scoring, and the Chrome/email extension; Individual Pro runs roughly $45 to $49 per month and adds third-party CRM and sales-engagement integrations plus priority support; and a Team plan, priced around $89 per user per month, adds team-wide coaching analytics, manager dashboards and org-level reporting, with roughly 20 percent savings typically available for annual billing and a 7-day free trial offered on paid tiers. Lavender's target audience is business-to-business sales development representatives, account executives, and sales leaders at technology and SaaS companies who send high volumes of cold or semi-personalized outbound email and want to systematically improve reply rates without hiring a dedicated sales-enablement coach for every rep; it is also used by revenue-operations and sales-enablement teams that want visibility into which messaging patterns correlate with pipeline generation across an entire sales floor. Its competitive landscape includes Yesware, a longer-established sales engagement and email-tracking platform focused more on send/open tracking and campaign automation than AI coaching; Outreach and Salesloft, comprehensive sales-engagement platforms that manage entire multi-channel outbound sequences and are typically deployed by larger sales organizations, of which Lavender is often used as a complementary coaching layer rather than a replacement; and Gong, a revenue-intelligence platform that analyzes recorded sales calls rather than written email, overlapping with Lavender only at the margins of the broader 'sales AI coaching' category. Lavender differentiates itself by focusing narrowly and deeply on the moment of email composition itself, providing instant, actionable, line-level feedback inside the compose window rather than after-the-fact campaign analytics, which is why it is frequently adopted alongside rather than instead of full sales-engagement platforms like Outreach or Salesloft.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lavender, founded in 2020, is an AI email coaching platform built specifically for B2B sales teams, overlaying Gmail and Outlook with real-time scoring and rewrite suggestions as reps draft outbound emails.
The company created what it calls the 'email intelligence' category, raising $13.2 million in venture funding and counting customers such as Clari, Segment, Twilio and Sendoso.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Lavender's core feature is a real-time email score with line-by-line feedback on readability, tone, personalization, spelling, grammar and spam-trigger language as a rep types.
The platform also automates prospect and company research inside the compose window and provides manager-facing analytics dashboards showing coaching trends across a sales team.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: A limited free plan offers around five email analyses and personalizations per month, while Starter and Individual Pro plans unlock unlimited scoring, AI rewrites and CRM integrations for individual sellers.
The Team plan adds manager dashboards and org-wide coaching analytics at a per-user price, with roughly 20 percent savings on annual billing and a 7-day free trial on paid tiers.

Complements rather than replaces existing sales-engagement platforms like Outreach or Salesloft

Strong customer base among recognizable B2B SaaS companies
7-day free trial available on paid plans

faqs.question: Is Lavender free to use?
faqs.answer: Lavender offers a limited free plan with around five email analyses and five personalizations per month; unlimited access requires a paid Starter, Pro or Team plan.

faqs.question: What does Lavender's email score measure?
faqs.answer: Lavender scores emails on readability, tone, length, personalization, spelling and grammar, and spam-trigger language, providing line-by-line feedback to improve reply rates.

faqs.question: Does Lavender integrate with my CRM?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lavender integrates with Gmail, Outlook, and sales tools like Salesforce, HubSpot, Salesloft and Outreach, with deeper integrations available on the Pro and Team plans.

faqs.question: How much does Lavender cost for teams?
faqs.answer: Lavender's Team plan is priced at around $89 per user per month and adds manager dashboards and org-wide coaching analytics.

faqs.question: Who uses Lavender?
faqs.answer: Lavender is used primarily by B2B sales development reps, account executives and sales leaders who send high volumes of outbound email and want to improve reply rates.

faqs.question: How is Lavender different from Outreach or Salesloft?
faqs.answer: Outreach and Salesloft manage entire multi-channel outbound sequences, while Lavender focuses specifically on coaching the quality of individual email drafts, and is often used alongside those platforms.

enrichment.short_description: Lavu is an iPad-based restaurant point-of-sale system offering order management, kitchen displays, online ordering, payments and payroll tools.
enrichment.seo_title: Lavu Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lavu review 2026: iPad restaurant POS features, pricing tiers, and how it compares to Toast, Square for Restaurants and other POS alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Lavu is an iPad-based restaurant point-of-sale (POS) and restaurant-management platform founded in 2010 and headquartered in Albuquerque, New Mexico, built as one of the earlier tablet-native alternatives to legacy, proprietary restaurant hardware terminals from vendors like Micros and Aloha. The company raised a $15 million growth funding round in 2015 led by Aldrich Capital Partners to expand its restaurant customer base and hardware/payments infrastructure, and while some more recent company profiles describe Lavu as having operated largely without additional outside institutional investment since then, it has continued to expand its product line, including launching Lavu Capital in 2022, a merchant-financing product powered by fintech partner Parafin that offers restaurant operators access to working-capital advances based on their processing history. Lavu employs roughly 192 people and serves a customer base skewed toward independent and small-chain restaurants, with reported figures suggesting roughly a quarter of its customers are small operations under 50 employees and a smaller share are large, multi-location enterprise accounts. Architecturally, Lavu runs on Apple iPads as the primary point-of-sale terminal, paired with a cloud-based back office that syncs menu, inventory and sales data in real time across single- or multi-location restaurants, and it supports offline mode so terminals can keep taking orders during internet outages and sync once connectivity is restored. The platform bundles core POS order-taking and table/tab management with a kitchen display system (KDS) that routes tickets directly to kitchen screens instead of paper printers, integrated payment processing, online ordering and delivery integrations, menu and inventory management, employee scheduling and payroll tools, and customer relationship/loyalty features; more recently Lavu has introduced an AI assistant branded 'Marty AI' that surfaces sales insights, automates parts of back-office reporting, and helps operators make faster menu or staffing decisions from natural-language queries. Pricing is largely quote-based rather than fully self-serve and transparent online, reflecting the customized hardware-plus-software-plus-payments bundle typical of restaurant POS vendors; third-party pricing trackers report entry packages starting around $59 to $69 per month for a single terminal with core POS features, sometimes bundled with discounted or free hardware, scaling through mid-tier packages in the $129 to $249 per month range as restaurants add terminals or users, up to a higher-end 'Optimize' package reported around $279 per month, billed annually, for full-featured, multi-location operations; actual pricing depends on number of terminals, add-on modules selected, and negotiated payment-processing rates, so prospective customers typically need to request a custom quote or demo rather than self-checkout online. Lavu's target audience is independent restaurants, quick-service and fast-casual chains, pizzerias, bars and small multi-location restaurant groups that want an iPad-native POS with integrated payments and back-office tools without the higher price point or heavier enterprise sales process of some larger competitors. Its principal competitors are Toast, the current market leader in restaurant POS, known for its Android-based proprietary hardware, extensive third-party integration ecosystem and larger enterprise/franchise focus; Square for Restaurants, which leverages Square's broader payments ecosystem and appeals to smaller operators and multi-industry merchants already using Square elsewhere; and Upserve and SpotOn, which compete similarly on iPad/tablet-based restaurant POS with integrated payments. Lavu differentiates itself as one of the original iPad-first restaurant POS platforms, generally positioning on flexibility and price relative to Toast's more hardware-locked, higher-cost ecosystem, while competing hardest for the small-to-midsize independent restaurant segment rather than large national chains.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lavu, founded in 2010 in Albuquerque, New Mexico, was one of the earliest iPad-native restaurant point-of-sale systems, built as a lower-cost, tablet-based alternative to legacy proprietary POS hardware.
The platform now spans order-taking, kitchen display, payments, online ordering, payroll and financing through its Lavu Capital product, aimed primarily at independent and small-chain restaurants.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Lavu's core POS runs on iPads with a cloud-synced back office, offline mode for connectivity outages, and a kitchen display system that routes tickets directly to the kitchen.

Additional tools cover online ordering, payment processing, inventory and menu management, employee scheduling and payroll, plus an AI assistant called Marty AI for natural-language sales and operations insights.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Lavu's pricing is largely quote-based, with third-party trackers reporting entry packages starting around $59-69/month per terminal, scaling up through mid-tier options as restaurants add terminals or users.

Higher-end packages such as the reported 'Optimize' tier run roughly $279/month billed annually for full-featured, multi-location restaurant operations, with final pricing depending on a custom quote.

features.title: iPad-native POS
features.description: Runs on Apple iPads as the primary terminal, paired with a cloud-synced back office for menu, inventory and sales data.

features.title: Kitchen display system
features.description: Routes order tickets directly to kitchen screens instead of paper printers, reducing errors and speeding up service.

features.title: Offline mode
features.description: Terminals continue taking orders during internet outages and sync automatically once connectivity is restored.

features.title: Integrated payment processing
features.description: Built-in payment processing handles card and contactless transactions directly through the POS.

features.title: Online ordering and delivery integrations
features.description: Connects in-house online ordering with third-party delivery platforms alongside in-store orders.

features.title: Employee scheduling and payroll
features.description: Manages staff schedules and payroll directly within the platform.

features.title: Marty AI assistant
features.description: AI assistant that surfaces sales insights and automates back-office reporting from natural-language queries.

features.title: Lavu Capital financing
features.description: Offers restaurant operators working-capital advances powered by fintech partner Parafin, based on processing history.

pros: One of the original iPad-native restaurant POS platforms with a mature feature set
Offline mode keeps service running during internet outages
Bundles payroll and scheduling alongside core POS functionality
Lavu Capital provides built-in access to working-capital financing
Generally positioned as more affordable than Toast for independent restaurants
cons: Pricing is not fully transparent online and typically requires a custom quote
Smaller market share and integration ecosystem compared to Toast and Square
iPad dependency ties hardware choice to Apple's ecosystem and pricing
Reported starting prices vary across third-party sources, adding some uncertainty
Less brand recognition among large multi-location enterprise chains

faqs.question: How much does Lavu cost?
faqs.answer: Lavu's pricing is largely quote-based, with third-party trackers reporting entry packages starting around $59-69 per month per terminal, scaling up based on terminals, users and features selected.

faqs.question: Does Lavu work offline?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lavu supports an offline mode that lets terminals keep taking orders during internet outages, syncing data once connectivity is restored.

faqs.question: What is Marty AI?
faqs.answer: Marty AI is Lavu's built-in AI assistant that surfaces sales insights and automates parts of back-office reporting from natural-language queries.

faqs.question: What is Lavu Capital?
faqs.answer: Lavu Capital, launched in 2022 and powered by fintech partner Parafin, offers restaurant operators working-capital financing based on their payment processing history.

faqs.question: What hardware does Lavu run on?
faqs.answer: Lavu runs primarily on Apple iPads as the point-of-sale terminal, paired with a cloud-based back office.

faqs.question: How does Lavu compare to Toast?
faqs.answer: Lavu is generally positioned as a more affordable, iPad-native alternative to Toast, which uses proprietary Android-based hardware and has a larger enterprise/franchise focus.

use_cases.title: Independent restaurant point-of-sale
use_cases.description: Independent restaurants use Lavu's iPad POS, kitchen display and payment processing to run day-to-day front- and back-of-house operations.

use_cases.title: Multi-location quick-service management
use_cases.description: Small restaurant groups and quick-service chains use Lavu's cloud back office to manage menus, inventory and reporting across multiple locations.

use_cases.title: Restaurant working-capital financing
use_cases.description: Operators use Lavu Capital to access working-capital advances based on their processing history without applying for a traditional bank loan.
